evidence.title: IDSA Updates Guideline for Managing Group A Streptococcal Pharyngitis | AAFP
evidence.snippet: Drug Dose/dosage Duration Recommendation strength, quality of evidence Patients without penicillin allergy Penicillin V, oral Children: 250 mg two or three times daily 10 days Strong, high Adolescents and adults: 250 mg four times daily or 500 mg twice daily Amoxicillin, oral 50 mg per kg once daily (maximum = 1,000 mg) 10 days Strong, high Alternative: 25 mg per kg twice daily (maximum= 500 mg) Penicillin G benzathine, intramuscular Single dose Strong, high ≥ 60 lb: 1,200,000 U Patients with pencillin allergy Cephalexin (Keflex), oral * 20 mg per kg per dose twice daily (maximum = 500 mg per dose) 10 days Strong, high Cefadroxil, oral * 30 mg per kg once daily (maximum = 1 g) 10 days Strong, high Clindamycin, oral 7 mg per kg per dose three times daily (maximum = 300 mg per dose) 10 days Strong, moderate Azithromycin (Zithromax), oral † 12 mg per kg once daily (maximum = 500 mg) 5 days

evidence.title: Clinical Guidance for Group A Streptococcal Pharyngitis | Group A Strep | CDC
evidence.snippet: Cephalexin, oral 20 mg/kg/dose twice daily (maximum = 500 mg/dose) for 10 days Cefadroxil, oral 30 mg/kg once daily (maximum = 1 g) for 10 days Clindamycin, oral 7 mg/kg/dose 3 times daily (maximum = 300 mg/dose) for 10 days Azithromycin, oral 12 mg/kg once (maximum = 500 mg), then 6 mg/kg (max=250 mg) once daily for the next 4 days Clarithromycin, oral 7.5 mg/kg/dose twice daily (maximum = 250 mg/dose) for 10 days Antibiotic resistance There's never been a report of a clinical isolate of group A strep bacteria that's resistant to penicillin or cephalosporins.

evidence.title: Strep throat: MedlinePlus Medical Encyclopedia
evidence.snippet: Certain other antibiotics may also work against the strep bacteria. Antibiotics should be taken for 10 days, even though symptoms are often gone within a few days. The following tips may help your sore throat feel better:

note_text:
S: The patient is a 47-year-old female presenting with a 2-day history of sore throat accompanied by fever, chills, swollen glands, and white patches on the tonsils. She denies cough, nasal congestion, difficulty swallowing, or breathing. She reports recent exposure to her son who had strep throat last week. These symptoms and exposure history are consistent with streptococcal pharyngitis.

O: Phone consultation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: The working diagnosis is streptococcal pharyngitis given the acute onset of sore throat, fever, tonsillar exudates, cervical lymphadenopathy, and known exposure to a confirmed case.
Differential:
1. Streptococcal pharyngitis
2. Viral pharyngitis
3. Infectious mononucleosis
4. Other bacterial pharyngitis

P: - First-line medication: Azithromycin 500 mg orally on day 1, then 250 mg orally daily on days 2 through 5 (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
- Alternatives: Clindamycin 300 mg orally three times daily for 10 days (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
- Medication safety considerations: Patient has a penicillin allergy; avoid penicillin and amoxicillin. Monitor for gastrointestinal side effects with macrolides or clindamycin. Other contraindications not assessed in this telephone encounter.
- Follow-up recommendations: Seek in-person medical care urgently if difficulty breathing, severe swallowing difficulty, inability to maintain hydration, or worsening symptoms occur. Seek emergency care if sudden onset of airway obstruction or severe systemic symptoms develop.
- Testing: Arrange for rapid antigen detection test or throat culture for group A streptococcus if feasible to confirm diagnosis and guide therapy.
